nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, warm, slow-moving llanos streams and gallery-forest pools; soft, tannin-stained water over fine sand and leaf litter; upper Meta River drainage including the Río Ariari, Río Guayabero, and associated tributaries

decorPreferences: multiple small caves (coconut huts, clay pots, PVC sections)
Indian almond or catappa leaves for tannins and surface cover
dense planting along sides and background (Java fern, Anubias, Amazon sword)
low open areas in the foreground for territorial display

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild; feeds on small invertebrates, microcrustaceans, worms, and insect larvae. In the aquarium, readily accepts live and frozen foods: baby brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworms, tubifex, and cyclops. Quality micro-pellets and small granules are generally accepted by established specimens. Feed small portions two to three times daily.

goodWith: small tetras (cardinal tetra, rummy nose tetra, ember tetra)
corydoras catfish (small species: C. habrosus, C. pygmaeus)
avoidWith: other male Apistogramma macmasteri or conspecific males without adequate tank size and territory separation
boisterous fast-moving fish that stress the pair
cherry shrimp and small dwarf shrimp (likely to be predated, especially fry-guarding female)

imageCandidates.notes: Dnoerholm; 4383x2922. Two males in full Red Mask nuptial colour — vivid red face and forebody, extended dorsal, yellow lyre-shaped caudal with red edging and bold lateral markings; sharp, high-res, shows all diagnostic features.
Cupr78up; 2000x1500. Colourful male showing red-orange facial mask with blue cheek markings, gold dorsal and lateral stripe; slightly soft in the fins but face and body clear.
Jorsha; 2038x1157. Full-body male in gold form with distinct yellow lyre-shaped caudal fin, lateral stripe and caudal-peduncle spot; somewhat soft/hazy so kept as a variety extra, not the pick.

summary: Macmaster's Dwarf Cichlid is a Colombian llanos species known for its striking color-form diversity, including the Red Mask, Red Neck, and Gold morphs. Males develop a pointed, lyre-shaped tail and vivid red facial markings. The species is frequently confused with Apistogramma viejita in the trade.
careNotes:
Apistogramma macmasteri is endemic to the Colombian Llanos, primarily collected from the upper Meta River drainage (Río Ariari, Río Guayabero). Wild habitat is shallow, warm, slow-moving llanos streams and forest-edge pools with soft, acidic, tannin-stained water over fine sand and leaf litter. The species shows strong color-form diversity in the trade: the 'Red Mask' morph features an extensive red blush over the face and opercula; 'Red Neck' displays red coloration across the nape and dorsal-anterior body; the 'Gold' or 'Yellow' variant shows warm yellow-gold body tones. These trade forms largely reflect collection locality variation and selective captive breeding rather than distinct subspecies.

A. macmasteri is commonly confused with Apistogramma viejita in the hobby — both originate in Colombia and are sold interchangeably under informal trade names. The two can be distinguished by the suborbital stripe: A. macmasteri carries a bold, well-defined lateral stripe that terminates as a prominent dark spot at the caudal peduncle, while A. viejita's lateral stripe tends to be less distinct and its caudal spot smaller or absent. Males of A. macmasteri also typically develop a more pointed, lyrate caudal fin than A. viejita.

In the wild, A. macmasteri forms harems, and it is best kept as one male with two or three females in a tank of 75–100 L with a footprint of at least 60 × 40 cm. Each female requires her own cave spawning site and defined territory; insufficient caves will result in aggressive female-on-female competition. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 5.5–7.0, gH 1–10 dGH) is strongly recommended, with tannin enrichment via catappa leaves or driftwood. Captive-bred strains tolerate neutral to slightly hard water more readily than wild-caught fish, but spawning success and coloration are generally improved in soft, acidic conditions. Females turn vivid yellow when spawning or guarding fry — a reliable behavioral indicator. The male should be removed from smaller tanks if he harasses the brooding female.
breedingNotes: Cave spawner with strong maternal brood care typical of the genus. The female selects and cleans a cave site, lays 40–80 eggs on the ceiling or walls, and defends the territory aggressively. Eggs are typically orange-red and hatch in 48–60 hours at 26°C. Free-swimming fry emerge after a further 4–5 days and are guarded closely by the female for 2–3 weeks. In smaller tanks the male may be harassed or overly stress the female; a divider or temporary removal is advisable. First foods: freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and microworms. Breeding is considered moderately challenging — it requires soft, acidic water and multiple cave options for best results, though captive-bred strains will spawn in near-neutral water. Color forms breed true with selective pairing.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma macmasteri
primary.notes: Primary husbandry and taxonomic reference. Confirmed native range (Meta drainage, Colombia), water parameters (pH 4.5–7.0, dH 1–10, 23–28°C), adult size (males to 9 cm, females to 5 cm), cave-spawning breeding, female brood care, and trade confusion with A. viejita. Order updated to Cichliformes per FishBase modern classification.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma macmasteri
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichliformes / Cichlidae), native range (Meta River drainage, Colombia), standard length (males ~8 cm SL), temperature range (23–27°C), and diet (benthic invertebrates). Used Cichliformes order as modern authority.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Dwarf Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma guide supplemented macmasteri-applicable trade guidance: tank sizing (20 gal / ~75 L for a pair), water column temperature (26–27°C), pH (slightly acidic to neutral), diet (live/frozen micro-foods), cave requirements, and harem keeping notes. No dedicated A. macmasteri page available; general guidance applied.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Apistogramma macmasteri profile
additional.notes: Confirmed origin in Colombian llanos streams (Meta drainage), color-form diversity (Red Mask, Gold), trade confusion with A. viejita, suborbital stripe as distinguishing character, soft acidic preferred parameters, harem behavior, and cave-spawning broodcare notes.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Apistogramma macmasteri species profile and trade notes
additional.notes: Cross-referenced A. macmasteri vs. A. viejita distinguishing morphology (caudal spot size, suborbital stripe clarity, lyrate caudal fin in macmasteri males), color form descriptions (Red Mask, Red Neck, Gold), spawning clutch size (~40–80 eggs), fry behavior, and recommendations for multiple cave sites per female.

fish.breedingNotes: A cave spawner with strong female brood care. Soft, acidic water and multiple cave sites are needed for reliable spawning. The female lays a clutch of 40–80 orange-red eggs and guards the fry for 2–3 weeks post-hatch. First foods for the fry are brine shrimp nauplii and microworms.
